report:
TerraVivo Renewables – Private Equity Research Memo
Date: June 1, 2026

I. Company Overview and Business Model

– TerraVivo Renewables, Inc. is a U.S.-based developer and operator of utility-scale solar and energy storage projects. Founded in 2016, headquartered in Austin, Texas.
– Privately held, currently seeking growth equity investment for expansion into international markets.
– Business model: end-to-end renewable energy project development, including site acquisition, permitting, engineering, construction oversight, and long-term asset management and power sales.
– Revenue streams:
  • Project development fees from customers (utilities, corporate buyers, IPPs).
  • Recurring operations and maintenance (O&M) contracts.
  • Long-term Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) from owned/operated solar+storage assets (circa 25% of revenue).
– Key metrics:
  • 2025 Revenue: $185M (up 65% YoY from $112M in 2024).
  • Gross margin: ~30%, improving from 27% in 2024 due to higher-margin O&M and asset ownership mix.
  • EBITDA margin: 18% in 2025 (vs. 12% in 2024).
  • Backlog pipeline: $1.1B of contracted and early-stage development opportunities through 2028.
– 2025 installed capacity under management: 1.2 GW solar, 400 MWh battery storage, servicing primarily US markets, with initial entry into Mexico in 2025.
– Employee count: 420, with ~180 in engineering and project management.

II. Market Context and Competitive Landscape

– The US utility-scale solar+storage market is rapidly expanding, with ~20 GW installed 2025E and ~25% annual growth forecast through 2030.
– TerraVivo is positioned as a mid-market specialist focused on complex solar+storage integrated projects, combining renewable generation with multi-hour battery systems, addressing peak-shaving and grid reliability demands.
– Competitors:
  • Large engineers and EPC firms (AES Clean Energy, First Solar EPC).
  • Specialized development firms (BrightLeaf Energy, Helios Solutions).
  • Emerging energy asset managers transitioning into project development.
– TerraVivo differentiates itself by:
  • Deep technical expertise in hybrid solar+storage system design.
  • Strong regulatory relationships and permitting track record in Texas, California, and select Midwestern states.
  • Integrated post-build asset management platform delivering stable cash yields.
– Market barriers: increasing project complexity, regulatory hurdles, and supply chain constraints.

III. Customer Base

– Customers include large utilities (TXU Energy, PG&E), corporate off-takers (Amazon, Walmart), independent power producers, and community energy cooperatives.
– Customer retention is high; long-term contracts exceed 5 years on average.
– Revenue concentration: top 10 customers represent ~45% of revenues, but diversified across sectors and geographies.
– Customer feedback indicates TerraVivo’s strong execution reliability and technical capabilities.

IV. Financial Summary

(in millions USD, fiscal year ends Dec 31)

| Metric                  | 2023   | 2024   | 2025 (proj) |
|-------------------------|--------|--------|-------------|
| Revenue                 | $58.2M | $112.4M| $185.3M     |
| Gross profit            | $15.6M | $30.2M | $55.3M      |
| Gross margin            | 26.8%  | 26.9%  | 29.8%       |
| EBITDA                  | $4.2M  | $13.5M | $33.3M      |
| EBITDA margin           | 7.2%   | 12.0%  | 18.0%       |
| Net income (est.)       | $1.1M  | $6.2M  | $14.9M      |
| Cash balance            | $8.9M  | $17.7M | $26.5M      |
| Total debt              | $12.0M | $10.8M | $15.5M      |

– Growth fueled by increased project wins, improved operational efficiency, and rising percentage of recurring revenue (O&M and asset ownership).
– Conservative leverage and strong cash flow generation enable continued growth without excessive external financing.

V. Growth Opportunities

– Geographic expansion into Mexico and Brazil (markets with fast-growing renewables demand and nascent energy storage adoption).
– Expanding battery storage capacity adds differentiation and higher margin opportunities.
– Service line expansion: launch of digital asset management platform offering real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance.
– Pursuit of carve-out acquisitions of smaller regional development firms to bolster local permitting and customer relationships.
– Long-term PPA extensions and asset acquisitions to increase recurring revenue base.

VI. Risks & Mitigants

– Supply chain volatility for batteries and inverters could delay projects; company secured multi-year contracts with suppliers mitigating short-term risk.
– Regulatory changes at state or federal level may affect project economics; TerraVivo’s experienced regulatory affairs team helps navigate evolving policy.
– Customer concentration risk partly mitigated by diverse contract geographies and mix of corporate, utility and IPP clients.
– Competition pressures may intensify, but technical niche and reputation create defensible position.

VII. Summary

TerraVivo Renewables is a fast-growing, mid-market renewable energy developer/operator focused on utility-scale solar and energy storage projects. The company demonstrates strong financial growth, improving margins, healthy cash flow, and a robust project pipeline. Its technical expertise, deep customer relationships, and expansion into attractive new markets create a compelling investment opportunity with significant upside potential.

This memo is for informational purposes only and is not an investment recommendation.

---
reasoning:
Okay, looking at TerraVivo Renewables, this company operates in the booming solar plus battery storage industry, which is clearly high growth. Their 2025 projected revenue of $185 million, up 65% from prior year, shows very strong growth. EBITDA margin of 18% in 2025 also indicates improving profitability and operational leverage, which is a positive financial trend for a growth-stage company.

Competition is fierce with big EPCs and specialized developers, but TerraVivo’s niche expertise in hybrid solar+storage systems and deep regulatory know-how give them a defendable competitive advantage. That’s crucial given the complexity and regulatory hurdles in this sector.

Their customer base is diversified across utilities and large corporates with long-term contracts, which supports recurring and predictable revenue. Although the top 10 customers represent 45% revenue concentration, the mix across sectors and geographies helps mitigate concentration risk.

Financially, the company is generating positive EBITDA, improving net income, and growing cash balances while maintaining reasonable debt levels. The strong backlog of $1.1 billion project pipeline through 2028 indicates sustained future revenue visibility.

Growth prospects are excellent, including expansion into Latin American markets with growing renewables demand. Their plans to grow battery storage capacity and launch a digital asset management platform further suggest scalable, high-margin revenue streams. Potential tuck-in acquisitions would also support faster market penetration.

Risks such as supply chain disruption and regulatory changes exist, but TerraVivo appears to have credible mitigants through supplier contracts and regulatory expertise.

Overall, the company combines strong growth, improving profitability, differentiated capabilities, and tangible runway for expansion. These factors suggest a high conviction investment opportunity.

Therefore, I am leaning strongly towards recommending investment with a 'strong_yes' proceed signal, provided standard due diligence confirms assumptions.

verdict.evidence_pro:
1. Robust 65% projected YoY revenue growth in 2025 reaching $185M with improving EBITDA margins from 12% to 18%, demonstrating operational leverage.
2. Strong $1.1 billion project backlog through 2028 providing revenue visibility.
3. Unique technical expertise in complex hybrid solar+storage projects creates a defensible market niche.
4. Diversified and sticky customer base with long-term contracts across utilities, corporates, and IPPs minimizes revenue concentration risk.
5. Clear growth runway via geographic expansion into Latin America, battery capacity increases, digital platform launch, and bolt-on acquisition strategy.
6. Positive cash flow generation and manageable debt support financial flexibility for scale.
verdict.evidence_contra:
1. Customer revenue concentration remains moderately high with top 10 customers accounting for 45%, posing some dependency risk.
2. Supply chain risks for battery storage components could impact project delivery timelines.
3. Regulatory and policy environment remains fluid and potentially volatile in key markets.
4. Increasing competitive intensity from large EPCs and specialist developers may pressure margins.
5. Execution risk associated with international expansion and new service line deployments.
6. Moderate leverage on balance sheet due to growth financing requirements.
verdict.similar_deals:
• SilverRock Capital's $220 million growth equity investment in SunGrid Hybrid Solutions (2024) focusing on solar+storage project development in US and LATAM.
• GreenLeaf Partners' $180 million equity buyout of Helios Energy Engineering (2023), a mid-sized solar EPC with strong battery integration expertise.
• Summit Energy's $95 million add-on acquisition of BrightPath Renewables (2025), a regional solar developer expanding into energy storage.
• Evergreen Infrastructure's $150 million growth capital round in FlexStor Technologies (2024), a digital asset management platform for renewables assets.
verdict.key_information_requested:
• Detailed breakdown of project pipeline, including stage (permitting, construction, contracted) and geographic distribution.
• Evidence on supply chain contract terms and mitigation against component price volatility or shortages.
• Customer contract review to assess revenue stability, renewal rates, and concentration risk.
• Financial model sensitivity analyses around regulatory changes, battery costs, and project margins.
• Assessment of management team’s experience with international expansion and new service lines.
• Due diligence on liabilities and contingent risks related to long-term PPAs and asset ownership.
